sub_pixel_system.notes: No sub-pixel data documented for this puzzle game

special_mechanics.name: Capsule Falling
special_mechanics.description: Two-colored medical capsules (megavitamins) fall vertically into the playing field. Player can move left/right and rotate 90 degrees clockwise or counter-clockwise.

physics_values.fall_behavior: Capsules fall at configurable speeds (Low, Medium, High difficulty levels)
physics_values.unsupported_fall: Remaining half or whole capsules not supported fall to bottom or until hitting another object

special_mechanics.name: Gravity Physics (Puzzle Mode)
special_mechanics.description: After matching colors, unsupported capsule halves fall down. Early build option allowed disabling this (RAM $724).

physics_values.can_disable: Yes, via Game Genie codes or RAM modification

special_mechanics.name: Chain Reactions
special_mechanics.description: New 4-in-a-row alignments automatically disappear after initial matches, causing cascading eliminations

physics_values.game_boy_feature: Chain of 4+ plays Invincible Music from Super Mario Bros.
physics_values.scoring: Bonus points for clearing more than 1 virus in single line

tier_notes: Dr. Mario is a puzzle game without traditional character movement physics. No walk/run speeds, jump mechanics, or sub-pixel systems are applicable. Movement is limited to capsule manipulation (horizontal rotation, left/right placement). Sources document game mechanics, anti-piracy, and development history but contain no quantitative movement physics data.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Dr. Mario NES article)
